bibliographic.citation: Lanfranc of Milan, Lanfrank's ‘Science of Cirurgie,’ ed. Robert von Fleischhacker, Early English Text Society, Original Series 102 (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co., 1894).
bibliographic.pages: 360 pages, with preliminary title, correction sheet, and blanks
bibliographic.publisher: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co. for the Early English Text Society
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 102
bibliographic.title: Lanfrank's ‘Science of Cirurgie’

entities.label: Fleischhacker 1894 EETS edition of Science of Cirurgie
notes: The volume states ‘Part I—Text’ and ‘No more published.’

Pages 1-157 print Ashmole 1396 and Additional 12056 streams; p. 158 onward continues Ashmole only. Bracketed restorations and the pp. 258-261 Latin supply make the edition more than a purely diplomatic parallel transcription.

rights.basis: U-M's current item page applies No Copyright-United States to the material, and Internet Archive metadata marks the Wellcome-sourced 1894 volume with the Public Domain Mark.
rights.component: The 1894 edition, transcription, notes, apparatus, typography, and correction sheet

entities.description: A fourteenth-century English parchment codex containing a Middle English translation of Lanfranc's Chirurgia magna and a following group of recipes. Fleischhacker's near-complete principal stream maps the translation through ff. 1r-272v.

holding.notes: The official TEI catalog description identifies the Lanfranc Middle English translation and a following recipe item.

entities.label: Bodleian Library MS Ashmole 1396

physicalDescription.extent: Lanfranc translation transcribed by Fleischhacker through ff. 1r-272v; following recipes are outside the cataloged work
notes: Produced in England.
The current abbreviated online description derives from William Henry Black's 1845 Ashmole catalogue.

entities.description: A fifteenth-century vellum-and-paper medical manuscript containing a Middle English version of Lanfranc's larger surgical work, imperfect at the end. Fleischhacker prints its contents list and surviving text at ff. 31r-86v, ending in Treatise II chapter IV.

holding.notes: The current catalog exposes no digitized-content link and offers physical-item request access.

entities.description: A fourteenth-century Latin medical codex, probably produced in southern France. Lanfranc's Chirurgia magna occupies the component beginning at f. 14; Fleischhacker says his Latin supply on pp. 258-261 also uses this manuscript beginning at f. 71.

holding.notes: The current catalog exposes no digitized content and requires physical-item request procedures, including a letter of introduction.

entities.label: British Library Additional MS 26106

physicalDescription.extent: Lanfranc Chirurgia magna begins at f. 14 and is followed by Theodoric's Chirurgia at f. 110; Fleischhacker's cited passage begins at f. 71
notes: The manuscript was probably produced in southern France.
The exact endpoint and token-level contribution of its f. 71 passage to Fleischhacker pp. 258-261 remain unresolved.

entities.description: A 1546 Venice anthology of Latin surgical works printed by the Giunta press. The Lanfranc Chirurgia magna component begins at fol. 207r and ends at fol. 261r; Fleischhacker's supplied Latin begins at fol. 239v.

holding.notes: The BSB digital object supplies a complete 776-canvas facsimile and page-level OCR.

entities.description: A c. 1475 medical manuscript whose anonymous surgical compilation at ff. 10r-128r was completed in 1392 and embeds selected passages from the Science of Cirurgie translation tradition. It is an indirect, non-contiguous reuse rather than a complete direct witness.

holding.notes: The current catalog provides a complete digital object and detailed component description.

entities.label: Wellcome Collection MS 564

physicalDescription.extent: 201 leaves; the 1392 surgical compilation is at ff. 10r-128r
notes: The codex contains multiple medical works; only non-contiguous passages within the 1392 compilation reuse the target translation.
physicalDescription.support: Paper, except the final vellum leaf

access.entityLabel: Wellcome Collection complete digital facsimile of MS 564

localCopies.label: Complete Wellcome MS 564 PDF
notes: PDF integrity and 423-page count were verified.

workPortion.label: Non-contiguous Science of Cirurgie-derived passages in the 1392 surgical compilation
locators: host compilation ff. 10r-128r = provider PDF pages 30-266 = IIIF canvases 29-265
identified target parallels: Pars I.i.2-7, I.iii.10, II.i.1-20, and II.ii.1, dispersed within the host compilation
verified example f. 12r = PDF page 34 = IIIF canvas 33: bone-regeneration passage from Science of Cirurgie I.iii.4
verified example f. 64r = PDF page 138 = IIIF canvas 137: modified remedy passage from Science of Cirurgie I.iii.3
verified example f. 74v = PDF page 159 = IIIF canvas 158: repeated bone-regeneration passage from Science of Cirurgie I.iii.4
verified example f. 78v and following = PDF page 167 / IIIF canvas 166 onward: adapted contusion passage from Science of Cirurgie I.iii.5
verified example f. 90v = PDF page 191 = IIIF canvas 190: Lanfranc haemorrhage exemplum from Science of Cirurgie I.iii.9
verified example ff. 96r-96v = PDF pages 202-203 = IIIF canvases 201-202: rewritten spasm exemplum based on Science of Cirurgie I.iii.15
verified example f. 107v = PDF page 225 = IIIF canvas 224: ulcer-remedy adaptation based on Science of Cirurgie I.iii.11
verified Lanfranc-attributed example f. 111v = PDF page 233 = IIIF canvas 232
notes: The complete codex is cached. These locators identify indirect selected reuse and do not imply that all ff. 10r-128r reproduce the target work.

Colafrancesco's comparison table identifies target sections but does not publish exhaustive manuscript start and end folios for every non-contiguous excerpt. The listed folio-to-PDF/canvas pairs are the exact examples located from her notes and directly checked in the complete facsimile; a full passage-boundary inventory remains unresolved.

subject.description: Table 1 and passage-level comparisons of the 1392 compilation against the Science of Cirurgie streams conventionally labeled O and Ad

reviewNotes: The Middle English work, Lanfranc's Latin source, physical witnesses, 1546 Latin print, Fleischhacker edition, U-M encoding, repository artifact, scans, OCR, catalogs, and scholarly studies remain separate layers.
The Ashmole contents-list trailer on printed p. 4 is not a witness ending: the Ashmole main text runs from p. 7 through all five treatises, an epilogue, and the final table on p. 360.

Additional MS 12056 is a parallel direct witness through Treatise II chapter IV and also supplies selected bracketed restorations; its complete preliminary index does not prove that the surviving copy was once complete.

The complete 1546 BSB bundle contains 776 canvases at a documented 1200-pixel-width derivative profile. The cataloged Middle English work is absent; only a Latin source passage at fols. 239v-240v, canvases 494-496, is connected by a scoped edition relation.

Wellcome MS 564 is an indirect reception witness: excerpts from the same translation tradition are embedded non-contiguously in another compilation. No direct copied-from relation to Ashmole or Additional 12056 is asserted.
Current U-M No Copyright-United States language and the conditional 2006 availability notice embedded in the XML are both preserved rather than silently reconciled.

Colafrancesco's comparison table identifies target sections but does not publish exhaustive manuscript start and end folios for every non-contiguous excerpt. The listed folio-to-PDF/canvas pairs are the exact examples located from her notes and directly checked in the complete facsimile; a full passage-boundary inventory remains unresolved.

lineage.summary: The repository artifact is a copy or version of University of Michigan electronic item AHA2727, encoded from Robert von Fleischhacker's 1894 EETS edition. Fleischhacker prints the near-complete Middle English translation in Bodleian MS Ashmole 1396 and a second, partial copy in British Library Additional MS 12056 as parallel streams through printed p. 157, then continues Ashmole through p. 360. His edition is composite at selected points: bracketed Ashmole restorations use Additional 12056, while a lacuna on pp. 258-261 is supplied in Latin from the 1546 Venice Ars chirurgica anthology and Additional MS 26106. Complete scans of the 1894 edition, the 1546 anthology, and Wellcome MS 564 are cached; the latter preserves indirect selected reuse in a different 1392 surgical compilation and is not modeled as a direct complete third witness.

catalogSummary.title: Science of Cirurgie

catalogingSubject.label: Science of Cirurgie
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged intellectual unit is one anonymous late-fourteenth-century Middle English translation of Lanfranc of Milan's Latin Chirurgia magna, organized in five treatises. Fleischhacker's 1894 edition prints the near-complete Ashmole MS 1396 text and, through Treatise II chapter IV, a second witness in Additional MS 12056 as parallel representation streams; those streams are not two separate works. The 1894 editorial frame, Latin and English supplied passages, physical witnesses, digital scans, U-M encoding, and repository XML are separate transmission layers described in the linked lineage manifest.

contentParts.label: Science of Cirurgie
notes: Ashmole is near-complete rather than textually perfect: current scholarship identifies omission of the Latin prologue and sections III.iii.9, IV.i.1, and IV.ii.1.
contentParts.summary: The anonymous Middle English translation as a single five-treatise surgical work, represented near-completely by Ashmole MS 1396 and fragmentarily by Additional MS 12056.

contentParts.label: Treatise I: general surgery, anatomy, and wounds
contentParts.summary: Defines surgery and the surgeon's conduct, surveys anatomy, and treats wounds, bleeding, infection, and related complications.

contentParts.label: Treatise II: wounds by body part
contentParts.summary: Treats anatomy and wounds from the head downward through the face, neck, trunk, limbs, and feet.

contentParts.label: Treatise III: diseases and pathological conditions
contentParts.summary: Addresses diseases and surgical conditions organized across body regions and pathological types.

contentParts.label: Treatise IV: fractures and dislocations
contentParts.summary: Treats broken bones, dislocations, reduction, stabilization, and related care.

contentParts.label: Treatise V: antidotary
contentParts.summary: Collects medicinal preparations, compound remedies, and practical recipes used in surgical treatment.

contentParts.label: Ashmole MS 1396 representation
notes: The trailer ‘The Ashmole MS. breaks off here’ follows the preliminary contents list on p. 4 and does not mark the end of the Ashmole main text.
contentParts.summary: The near-complete witness-specific stream used as Fleischhacker's principal text, including an initial contents list, five treatises, epilogue, and final chapter table.

contentParts.label: Additional MS 12056 representation
contentParts.summary: A second witness-specific stream with a complete five-treatise contents list but surviving main text only through Treatise II, chapter IV.

assertion.notes: The evidence supports a late-fourteenth-century translation but not a precise year.

scope.description: Anonymous Middle English translation, distinguished from the dates of its surviving copies

assertion.notes: Fleischhacker's approximate 1380 date is a historical editorial assertion; the current Bodleian description gives only the fourteenth century.

dateStatements.display: Late fourteenth century, Bodleian Library MS Ashmole 1396

scope.description: Earliest surviving near-complete witness currently established for the translation

extent.description: One translated surgical work in five intellectual treatises. XML structure comprises two top-level witness representations, 10 DIV2, 39 DIV3, 363 P, 515 PB, and 661 folio milestones. These structural counts do not turn the two witness streams into two works and do not count editorial chapter divisions as independent items.

formStatements.description: The medical work and its five treatises are prose; no intellectual verse lines or verse groups occur in the encoding.

languageStatements.label: Latin
notes: The underlying Latin Chirurgia magna remains a distinct expression. Fleischhacker's editorial Latin supply on pp. 258-261 is excluded from this work-language assertion and is modeled only in lineage.

scope.description: Latin technical vocabulary, glosses, and short quotations embedded in the Middle English translation

notes: The intellectual boundary is one translated work with five sections; parallel witness columns, missing leaves, and editorial supplies are representation and transmission facts.
Ashmole's preliminary contents list stops after Treatise II chapter X, but the main Ashmole text continues through all five treatises and a final table.
Wellcome MS 564 preserves selected adapted excerpts within a different 1392 compilation and is kept as an indirect related source, not silently promoted to a direct complete witness.

The five-treatise intellectual structure and both witness representations are modeled completely at the chosen work-and-treatise granularity; the 39 encoded chapter-level divisions are summarized rather than repeated as separate content parts.
Fleischhacker's correction sheet and other source-supply interventions belong to edition and lineage layers, not to the intrinsic intellectual-content hierarchy.

next_steps: Consult current linguistic and textual scholarship beyond the two open studies used here.
Keep scribal dialect evidence for Ashmole and Additional 12056 separate from claims about the translator's origin.
openQuestions.question: Who translated the Chirurgia magna into Middle English, when within the late fourteenth century, and where?

next_steps: Request current codicological and reproduction information from the Bodleian, quoting MS Ashmole 1396 and both catalog ARKs.
Do not promote Fleischhacker's approximate 1380 date to a current catalog fact without newer evidence.
openQuestions.question: Can Ashmole MS 1396 be dated more narrowly than the current catalog's fourteenth century, and is a complete public digital facsimile available?

next_steps: Request reproduction or conservation information from the British Library for ff. 31r-86v.
Treat the complete contents list as evidence of planned structure, not proof that the surviving copy was once complete.
openQuestions.question: Was Additional MS 12056 originally a complete copy of the translation, and can a public or requestable facsimile clarify its lost ending?

next_steps: Extend Colafrancesco's passage-level comparison across every identified excerpt.
Do not model Wellcome MS 564 as copied directly from Ashmole 1396 or Additional 12056 without new evidence.
openQuestions.question: What exemplar or contaminated branch supplied the Science of Cirurgie excerpts embedded in the 1392 surgical compilation copied in Wellcome MS 564?

next_steps: Ask U-M Digital Collections for written clarification before redistributing the U-M-derived transcription or markup.
Keep the medieval text, 1894 edition, modern page images, and U-M encoding rights layers separate.

scope.description: The late-fourteenth-century English translation transmitted principally by Ashmole MS 1396 and Additional MS 12056
Fleischhacker's 1894 parallel-witness edition, notes, collation, and bracketed restorations

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English prose translation of Lanfranc of Milan's Chirurgia magna. Its five treatises move from surgical principles, anatomy, and general wounds to wounds organized by body part, diseases and pathological conditions, fractures and dislocations, and an antidotary of medicinal preparations. The translation is substantially faithful yet adaptive and uses Middle English alongside Latin technical terminology.

The near-complete Ashmole stream represents all five treatises and a final chapter table. The Additional 12056 stream begins with a complete contents list but its surviving main text stops in Treatise II, chapter IV. Fleischhacker prints the streams in parallel through p. 157, then continues Ashmole alone through p. 360.

The two witness streams are representations of one translation, not separate works. The statement ‘The Ashmole MS. breaks off here’ on printed p. 4 closes only Ashmole's preliminary contents list; Ashmole's main text continues through all five treatises. Fleischhacker's edition is composite at selected points because it restores Ashmole readings from Additional 12056; other edition-layer interventions are documented in the lineage manifest.

notes: The established Middle English title appears in Fleischhacker's edition and modern scholarship; it is not attributed to Lanfranc's Latin work as an original title.
Catalog punctuation and capitalization are normalized from the 1894 title page.

The translation is substantially faithful yet adaptive and uses Middle English alongside Latin technical terminology.
